schools.summary: Gill: set after the plague upon Israel for the fornication and idolatry the daughters of Moab and Midian had drawn them into, and a little before the death of Moses.
Henry: the LORD of hosts giving orders to Moses to make war upon the Midianites.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses
the war on Midian after the plague of Peor
God settling his account with the seducers

schools.summary: Gill: the vengeance was for the injury of sending their daughters to entice Israel to uncleanness and idolatry, for which 24,000 perished; the Moabites, though concerned, were spared; afterward Moses should be gathered to his people.

themes: Avenge the children of Israel of the Midianites: afterward shalt thou be gathered unto thy people
the just vengeance for Peor
Moses' last work before his going home

schools.summary: Gill: he spake in obedience to the divine command, to the heads or princes of the tribes.

Clarke: it was God's quarrel, not their own; the Midianites were idolaters, idolatry an offence against God, and the civil power has no authority to meddle with what belongs to him without special command.

themes: Arm some of yourselves... to avenge the LORD of Midian
the LORD's quarrel, not Israel's
Israel the instrument of his judgment

schools.summary: Gill: the whole number armed was twelve thousand.
Keil & Delitzsch: a thousand of every tribe, throughout all the tribes of Israel, sent to the war.

themes: Of every tribe a thousand... shall ye send to the war
the whole nation sharing the cause
the modest number preparing God's glory

schools.summary: Gill: many likely offered themselves voluntarily, out of whom the chosen number was delivered.
Wesley: God would send no more, though the Midianites were numerous and strong, because he would exercise their faith.

themes: a thousand of every tribe, twelve thousand armed for war
the few against the many
God exercising their faith, the victory his

schools.summary: Gill: the army being mustered and armed was sent forth.
Clarke: the ark and its contents went to the battle, because the battle was the LORD's; Phinehas, who had before stayed the plague, went with them.

themes: Phinehas the son of Eleazar... with the holy instruments, and the trumpets to blow
the war waged as sacred, God its captain
the priest who stayed the plague completing the judgment

schools.summary: Gill: the Israelites committed acts of hostility, entering the cities, plundering, and slaying the inhabitants; they slew all the males as the judgment required.

themes: they warred against the Midianites, as the LORD commanded... and they slew all the males
the war an act of obedience
the execution of divine justice on Midian

schools.summary: Gill: the five kings were petty kings or princes, perhaps under the king of Moab; Balaam, who had counselled the seduction, was found among the Midianites and slain.
Keil & Delitzsch: name the five kings of Midian slain, and Balaam the son of Beor slain with the sword.

themes: Balaam also the son of Beor they slew with the sword
the deceiver perishing with those he counselled
the wages of unrighteousness reaped (2 Pet. 2:15; Jude 11)

schools.summary: Gill: the word 'all' is not in the original of the women, for not every one fell into their hands, else there could not have been the later increase of Midian; they took as many as came into their hands, with the cattle, flocks, and goods.

themes: the children of Israel took the women of Midian captives, and their little ones... and... goods
the wealth of Midian as spoil
the women spared, the occasion of Moses' anger

schools.summary: Gill: this was so far as they went into the country, perhaps not the whole land of Midian.
Ellicott: 'all their cities in their dwelling-places,' explained by the five chiefs of Midian who dwelt in the land (Josh. 13:21).

themes: they burnt all their cities... and all their goodly castles, with fire
the seats of the seducers laid waste
a thorough stroke of God's wrath

schools.summary: Gill: the spoil of the cities and castles, and the prey of men and beasts. Calvin: a sign of their disinterestedness and modesty, that they brought the booty taken in the ardour of battle to Moses and Eleazar, not as an empty show but in true subjection.

themes: they took all the spoil, and all the prey, both of men and of beasts
brought to Moses, not seized for themselves
the disinterestedness of the victors submitting to God

schools.summary: Gill: the women and children were the captives, the beasts the prey, and the gold and silver the spoil.
Keil & Delitzsch: brought to the camp in the plains of Moab by Jordan near Jericho.

themes: they brought the captives, and the prey, and the spoil, unto Moses, and Eleazar... and... the congregation
the fruits of victory the common possession
the orderly return keeping the triumph from strife

schools.summary: Gill: they went attended with a proper retinue.
Henry: Moses himself, despite his age and gravity, walked out of the camp to congratulate them on their victory and grace the solemnity.

themes: Moses, and Eleazar... and all the princes... went forth to meet them without the camp
the honour due to those who fight the Lord's battles
a welcome soon mingled with rebuke

schools.summary: Gill: his displeasure fell on the general officers, there being no one supreme general, else it would have fallen on him.
Keil & Delitzsch: Moses was wroth with the captains over thousands and hundreds who came from the battle.

themes: And Moses was wroth with the officers of the host
victory no excuse for a fault
the joy of triumph not silencing the rebuke

schools.summary: Gill: this is put not for information but by way of reproof, chiding them; they might have had orders to put them to death, and if so there was the more reason for anger.

themes: Have ye saved all the women alive?
the question a rebuke, not enquiry
misplaced mercy toward what God condemned

schools.summary: Gill: Balaam advised the Midianites to send their women into the camp of Israel to allure them to uncleanness and so to idolatry, which counsel succeeded; the plague came upon the congregation through this snare.

themes: these caused the children of Israel, through the counsel of Balaam, to commit trespass... in the matter of Peor
the deliberate instruments of the seduction
judged for their guilt, not their sex

schools.summary: Gill: this concerned the captives they had taken and brought.
Clarke: God, the author and supporter of life, has the right to dispose of it when and how he thinks proper; the Judge of all the earth can do nothing but what is right.

themes: kill every male among the little ones, and... every woman that hath known man
the guilty who wrought the seduction
the righteous Judge doing only what is right (Gen. 18:25)

schools.summary: Gill: these were the females among the little ones.
Ellicott: the Israelites might make these captives their own; the law concerning marriage with captives was shortly enacted, perhaps arising out of this (Deut. 21:10-14).

themes: all the women children, that have not known a man... keep alive
the innocent spared from the judgment
God providing for the protection of captives (Deut. 21:10-14)

schools.summary: Gill: seven days was the time anyone who touched a dead body remained unclean (Num. 19:11). Calvin: by extending the ceremony of expiation even to lawful homicide, Moses intimates how carefully we ought to abstain from shedding human blood.

themes: abide without the camp seven days... purify both yourselves and your captives
even lawful killing reckoned defiling
the cleansing pointing to the blood of Christ (Heb. 9:14)

schools.summary: Gill: this may intend chiefly the raiment taken off the dead bodies, since the one who merely touched a dead body was not obliged to wash his clothes; some render it 'purify yourselves together with the raiment'; skins, goats' hair, and wood to be cleansed.

themes: purify all your raiment, and all that is made of skins... and all things made of wood
the spoil cleansed before use
the people of God holy in all things

schools.summary: Gill: Eleazar spake to all the soldiers, officers and common men alike, teaching them the ordinance of the law the LORD commanded Moses.

themes: Eleazar the priest said unto the men of war... This is the ordinance of the law
the priest the teacher of the law (Mal. 2:7)
even the spoil disposed by God's rule

schools.summary: Gill: these metals, though unclean, were not to be purified by washing but in another way, by fire.
Keil & Delitzsch: the gold, silver, brass, iron, tin, and lead — the six metals of the spoil.

themes: Only the gold, and the silver, the brass, the iron, the tin, and the lead
cleansed by fire, not washing
the trial of fire refining what can bear it (1 Pet. 1:7)

schools.summary: Gill: what could abide the fire was to go through it, by suffumigation or melting, yet not consumed as cloth; what could not bear fire was cleansed with water.
Clarke: the 'water of separation' was the water in which the ashes of the red heifer were mingled (Num. 19:9).

themes: Every thing that may abide the fire... go through the fire... purified with the water of separation
fire that tries, water that washes
the twofold cleansing of death's defilement (Num. 19:17)

schools.summary: Gill: they washed their clothes on the seventh day, as the leper did (Lev. 14:9); only then might they come into the camp.

themes: ye shall wash your clothes on the seventh day, and ye shall be clean
the cleansing completed before return
none entering the camp while any defilement remained

schools.summary: Gill: this was after the purification of the soldiers, captives, and spoil.
Henry: God himself directed how the spoil should be distributed, so that unhappy contests among themselves were prevented and the victory turned to the common benefit.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses
God directing the division of the spoil
strife prevented, the victory a common benefit

schools.summary: Gill: the number of the females taken captive and spared, and of the cattle.
Keil & Delitzsch: Moses, Eleazar, and the chief fathers to take the sum of the prey, both of man and of beast.

themes: Take the sum of the prey that was taken, both of man and of beast
the prey numbered before dividing
the share exactly reckoned, none wronged

schools.summary: Gill: the prey was divided into two equal parts, both the people and the cattle.
Ellicott: reasonable that those who encountered the perils of war should receive a larger share per man, and that those who remained should not be left without a portion.

themes: divide the prey into two parts; between them that... went out to battle, and... all the congregation
the soldier honoured, the people not left out
the fruit of war justly shared (1 Sam. 30:24)

schools.summary: The tribute levied to the LORD from the warriors' spoil: 'and levy a tribute unto the LORD of the men of war which went out to battle: one soul of five hundred, both of the persons, and of the beeves, and of the asses, and of the sheep' — the LORD's portion — 'levy a tribute unto the LORD of the men of war... one soul of five hundred, both of the persons, and of the beeves, and of the asses, and of the sheep': a tribute, one in five hundred of the warriors' half of the spoil, levied to the LORD (the witnesses noting the significance — the acknowledgment that the victory and the spoil were God's gift, the tribute owning that the LORD gave the conquest, the first and best returned to him, the lesson — that God's people render to him a portion of what he gives, in thankful acknowledgment of his hand, the tribute the LORD's due from the spoil, the confession that the battle and the booty were his, the truth that all increase is God's gift, and a part is rendered back to him in homage, the figure — the firstfruits and the tribute, 'of all that thou shalt give me I will surely give the tenth unto thee,' Genesis 28:22, 'honour the LORD with thy substance, and with the firstfruits of all thine increase,' Proverbs 3:9, the smaller tribute from the warriors, one of five hundred, and the larger from the congregation, one of fifty, vv.30, the soldiers' portion lighter for their toil, the figure of the homage rendered to God from his gifts, the spoil acknowledged as his, the truth that the victory belongs to the LORD and is owned by the tribute, the figure of the tribute that owns the LORD's hand in the victory and his portion in the spoil); the tribute levied to the LORD from the soldiers' half (the LORD's due, the spoil his gift) — the levy (the tribute to God); the small portion, one of five hundred, from the warriors, the acknowledgment of God's gift (the homage, Genesis 28:22; Proverbs 3:9) — the figure (the firstfruits rendered to God, the providence, the tribute, the congregation's larger tribute to follow, vv.30) — Calvin, Ellicott, Clarke, Gill.

debates.question: What does the tribute levied to the LORD signify?

sides.position: The acknowledgment that the victory and the spoil were God's gift, a portion rendered back to him in homage (the warriors' lighter levy of one in five hundred, the congregation's heavier of one in fifty) - a figure of the firstfruits rendered to God, the confession that the battle and the booty belonged to the LORD (Genesis 28:22; Proverbs 3:9)

themes: And levy a tribute unto the LORD of the men of war which went out to battle: one soul of five hundred, both of the persons, and of the beeves, and of the asses, and of the sheep
the tribute levied to the LORD from the warriors' spoil (the crux of the LORD's portion and the acknowledgment of his hand in the victory)
the acknowledgment that the victory and the spoil were God's gift, a portion rendered back to him in homage
a figure of the firstfruits rendered to God, the confession that the battle and the booty belonged to the LORD (Genesis 28:22; Proverbs 3:9)

schools.summary: The tribute given to Eleazar the priest as a heave offering: 'take it of their half, and give it unto Eleazar the priest, for an heave offering of the LORD' — the tribute from the warriors' half given to Eleazar as a heave offering to the LORD (the take it of their half, from the soldiers' portion, the give it unto Eleazar the priest, to the high priest, as the LORD's representative, the for an heave offering of the LORD, the offering lifted up to God, the figure, the LORD's tribute given to the priest, who received it in God's name, the truth that what is rendered to God is given to his ministers, the heave offering to the priest, the figure of the portion of God passing to the priesthood, the holy tribute, the offering lifted up to the LORD and given to his servant, that the LORD's due be honored); the tribute from the warriors' half (the soldiers' portion) — the source (their half); 'give it unto Eleazar the priest, for an heave offering of the LORD' (the tribute given to the priest as a heave offering, the LORD's due to his minister, the figure, the holy tribute to the priesthood, the providence, the tribute to Eleazar, the congregation's tribute to the Levites to follow, vv.30) — Gill, Wesley.

themes: Take it of their half, and give it unto Eleazar the priest, for an heave offering of the LORD
the tribute given to Eleazar the priest as a heave offering
the warriors' tribute given to the high priest as a heave offering, the LORD's due passing to his minister
the holy portion honored

schools.summary: The congregation's larger tribute given to the Levites: 'and of the children of Israel's half, thou shalt take one portion of fifty, of the persons, of the beeves, of the asses, and of the flocks, of all manner of beasts, and give them unto the Levites, which keep the charge of the tabernacle of the LORD' — from the congregation's half a larger tribute, one of fifty, given to the Levites (the of the children of Israel's half, thou shalt take one portion of fifty, the larger tribute, one in fifty, the give them unto the Levites, which keep the charge of the tabernacle, to the Levites, the servants of the sanctuary, the figure, the congregation's heavier tribute given to the Levites, the soldiers' lighter to the priests, the truth that the people who did not fight rendered the larger portion, in acknowledgment of God's gift, the figure of the support of the Levites from the spoil, the ministry sustained by the people's gifts, 1 Corinthians 9:13-14, the LORD's due rendered, the lesser servants provided from the congregation's portion); the heavier tribute, one of fifty, from the congregation (the larger portion) — the tribute (one of fifty); 'give them unto the Levites, which keep the charge of the tabernacle' (the tribute given to the Levites, the ministry sustained, 1 Corinthians 9:13-14, the figure, the support of the Levites from the spoil, the providence, the tribute, the obedience in dividing to follow, vv.31) — JFB, Gill.

themes: And of the children of Israel's half, thou shalt take one portion of fifty... and give them unto the Levites, which keep the charge of the tabernacle of the LORD
the congregation's larger tribute given to the Levites
the congregation's heavier tribute, one of fifty, given to the Levites, the ministry sustained from the spoil
the LORD's portion rendered by the people (1 Corinthians 9:13-14)

schools.summary: Moses and Eleazar obeying the LORD's command in the division: 'and Moses and Eleazar the priest did as the LORD commanded Moses' — Moses and the high priest carried out the division of the spoil and the tribute exactly as God commanded (the Moses and Eleazar the priest did, the obedience, the as the LORD commanded Moses, the exact compliance, the refrain of obedience, the figure, the faithful obedience of Moses and Eleazar, doing all as God commanded, the truth that God's servants perform his word exactly, the division ordered by his command, the figure of the obedience that marks the leaders of God's people, the refrain 'as the LORD commanded,' the orderly carrying out of the LORD's directions for the spoil); Moses and Eleazar obeying (the faithful servants) — the obedience (they did); 'as the LORD commanded Moses' (the exact obedience, the refrain, the figure, the leaders performing God's word, the providence, the obedience, the numbering of the spoil to follow, vv.32) — Gill.

themes: And Moses and Eleazar the priest did as the LORD commanded Moses
Moses and Eleazar obeying the LORD's command in the division
the leaders carrying out the division of the spoil and the tribute exactly as God commanded
the obedience that marks God's servants

schools.summary: The great number of sheep in the spoil: 'and the booty, being the rest of the prey which the men of war had caught, was six hundred thousand and seventy thousand and five thousand sheep' — the spoil of sheep, after the slain, numbered six hundred seventy-five thousand (the the booty, being the rest of the prey, the spoil remaining, the six hundred thousand and seventy thousand and five thousand sheep, the great number of sheep, the figure, the vast spoil God gave from the Midianites, the abundance of the victory, the truth that God gave a rich booty, the spoil of the foe, the figure of the abundance of God's gift in the conquest, the great increase from the enemy, the wealth of the victory, the spoil to be divided and the LORD's tribute taken from it, vv.36-47); the spoil remaining after the slain (the booty) — the spoil (the prey caught); 'six hundred thousand and seventy thousand and five thousand sheep' (the great number of sheep, the abundance of the spoil, the figure, the rich booty God gave, the providence, the spoil numbered, the cattle and asses and persons to follow, vv.33-35) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the booty, being the rest of the prey which the men of war had caught, was six hundred thousand and seventy thousand and five thousand sheep
the great number of sheep in the spoil
the vast spoil God gave from the Midianites, the abundance of the victory
the rich booty to be divided and tithed to the LORD

schools.summary: The warriors' half of the sheep: 'and the half, which was the portion of them that went out to war, was in number three hundred thousand and seven and thirty thousand and five hundred sheep' — the warriors' half of the sheep was three hundred thirty-seven thousand five hundred (the the half, which was the portion of them that went out to war, the soldiers' share, the three hundred thousand and seven and thirty thousand and five hundred sheep, the number of the warriors' half, the figure, the spoil divided equally between the warriors and the congregation, the half to those who fought, the truth that the spoil was justly shared, the warriors and the people each a half, the figure of the equity of the division, the reward of the soldiers and the portion of the people, the order that the few who fought shared equally with the many who stayed, the spoil divided by God's command, vv.27); the warriors' half (the soldiers' portion) — the share (the warriors' half); 'three hundred thousand and seven and thirty thousand and five hundred sheep' (the number of the warriors' half, the equal division, the figure, the equity of the division, the providence, the warriors' half, the LORD's tribute from it to follow, vv.37) — JFB.

themes: And the half, which was the portion of them that went out to war, was in number three hundred thousand and seven and thirty thousand and five hundred sheep
the warriors' half of the sheep
the soldiers' equal share of the spoil, the warriors and the congregation each a half
the just division by God's command

schools.summary: The LORD's tribute of sheep from the warriors' half: 'and the LORD's tribute of the sheep was six hundred and threescore and fifteen' — the LORD's portion, one in five hundred of the warriors' half, came to six hundred seventy-five sheep (the the LORD's tribute of the sheep, the portion rendered to God, the six hundred and threescore and fifteen, the number, one of five hundred, the figure, the LORD's tribute exactly taken, the portion rendered to God from the warriors' half, the truth that the precise tribute was given, the LORD's due honored to the number, the figure of the homage rendered to God, the firstfruits of the spoil, the exact rendering of the LORD's portion, that his due be not withheld, the tribute given to the priest, vv.29); the LORD's portion of the sheep (the tribute) — the tribute (the LORD's due); 'six hundred and threescore and fifteen' (the exact tribute, one of five hundred, the precise rendering, the figure, the homage rendered to God, the providence, the tribute, the cattle and asses and persons to follow, vv.38-47) — Calvin.

themes: And the LORD's tribute of the sheep was six hundred and threescore and fifteen
the LORD's tribute of sheep from the warriors' half
the precise portion, one in five hundred, rendered to God from the warriors' half
the LORD's due honored to the number, the homage of the spoil

schools.summary: The congregation's half divided from the warriors': 'and of the children of Israel's half, which Moses divided from the men that warred' — Moses set apart the congregation's half of the spoil, distinct from the warriors' portion (the of the children of Israel's half, the congregation's share, the which Moses divided from the men that warred, the half set apart from the soldiers' portion, the figure, the spoil divided, the congregation's half set apart from the warriors', the truth that the people who did not fight shared equally in the spoil, the half theirs, the figure of the equity of the division, the people and the warriors each a half, the order that the spoil was justly shared, the congregation's portion distinct, from which the larger tribute to the Levites was taken, vv.30, 47); the congregation's half of the spoil (the people's portion) — the share (the people's half); 'which Moses divided from the men that warred' (the half set apart from the warriors', the just division, the figure, the equity of the division, the providence, the congregation's half, the Levites' tribute from it to follow, vv.47) — JFB.

themes: And of the children of Israel's half, which Moses divided from the men that warred
the congregation's half divided from the warriors'
the congregation's portion set apart from the warriors', the people and the soldiers each a half
the just division of the spoil

schools.summary: The officers of the host coming near to Moses: 'and the officers which were over thousands of the host, the captains of thousands, and captains of hundreds, came near unto Moses' — the commanders of the army came to Moses, to report and to bring an offering (the the officers which were over thousands of the host, the captains, the commanders, the the captains of thousands, and captains of hundreds, the officers of every rank, the came near unto Moses, their approach, the figure, the officers coming to Moses, to give account of the host and to bring their offering, the truth that the leaders came in thankfulness, owning God's mercy in the battle, the figure of the grateful approach of those whom God had preserved, the captains coming to acknowledge his goodness, the report of the muster and the freewill offering that followed, vv.49-50); the captains of the host (the officers) — the officers (the captains); 'came near unto Moses' (the officers approaching to report and offer, the grateful approach, the figure, the leaders owning God's mercy, the providence, the approach, the muster and the preservation to follow, vv.49) — Henry, Gill.

themes: And the officers which were over thousands of the host, the captains of thousands, and captains of hundreds, came near unto Moses
the officers of the host coming near to Moses
the captains coming to give account of the host and to bring their offering
owning God's mercy in the battle

schools.summary: The officers' report that not one man was missing after the battle: 'and they said unto Moses, Thy servants have taken the sum of the men of war which are under our charge, and there lacketh not one man of us' — the preservation — 'thy servants have taken the sum of the men of war which are under our charge, and there lacketh not one man of us': the officers, mustering the host after the battle, found not one soldier missing (the witnesses noting the significance — the wonderful preservation of all the warriors, the whole army returned, not one fallen, a marvel of God's protecting care in a great battle, the lesson — the keeping power of God over his people in the day of danger, the truth that God preserved every man, the victory won without a single loss, the hand of God plainly seen, the gratitude — the officers owning the mercy, moved to bring an offering in thankfulness, vv.50, the figure — the safekeeping of God's people, 'he keepeth all his bones: not one of them is broken,' Psalm 34:20, 'of them which thou gavest me have I lost none,' John 18:9, the truth that God so guards his own that not one is lost, the preservation of the flock by the Shepherd, the figure of the perfect keeping of God's people, not one missing, the victory of the LORD without loss, the gratitude that such mercy calls forth, the offering of thanksgiving, the figure of the wonderful preservation in which not one of God's people is lost); the officers numbering the host after the battle (the muster, the account given) — the muster (the sum taken); not one soldier missing, the wonderful preservation of God's people (the keeping power of God, the victory without loss, Psalm 34:20; John 18:9) — the figure (the perfect keeping of God's people, not one lost, the providence, the preservation, the thankful offering to follow, vv.50) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'there lacketh not one man of us' reveal?

sides.position: The wonderful preservation of God's people, the whole army returned from a great battle without a single loss - a marvel of God's protecting care owned with gratitude, a figure of the keeping power of God who so guards his own that not one is lost (Psalm 34:20; John 18:9), the perfect preservation of the flock by the Shepherd

themes: And they said unto Moses, Thy servants have taken the sum of the men of war which are under our charge, and there lacketh not one man of us
the officers' report that not one man was missing after the battle (the crux of the wonderful preservation of God's people)
the whole army returned from a great battle without a single loss, a marvel of God's protecting care
a figure of the keeping power of God who guards his own that not one is lost (Psalm 34:20; John 18:9)

schools.summary: The officers' freewill offering of gold to make atonement for their souls: 'we have therefore brought an oblation for the LORD, what every man hath gotten, of jewels of gold, chains, and bracelets, rings, earrings, and tablets, to make an atonement for our souls before the LORD' — the thankful atonement — 'we have therefore brought an oblation for the LORD, what every man hath gotten, of jewels of gold... to make an atonement for our souls before the LORD': the officers, in gratitude for their preservation, brought the gold of the spoil as an offering, to make atonement for their souls (the witnesses noting the significance — the thankful offering of the preserved, owning both God's mercy and their need of atonement, the gold rendered in gratitude for the wonderful preservation, vv.49, and as an atonement, that no sin in the war or the numbering be charged against them, the lesson — that the saved render to God in thankfulness and seek atonement, the offering both a thank offering for the mercy and an atonement for the soul, the gratitude that gives and the conscience that seeks cleansing, some noting the numbering of the people that needed a ransom, Exodus 30:12, 16, the atonement money for the soul, the figure — the thankful giving of those God has spared, and the atonement that covers the soul, 'ye were redeemed... with the precious blood of Christ,' 1 Peter 1:18-19, the true atonement for the soul made by Christ, of which this gold was but a shadow, the figure of the grateful offering and the atonement of the preserved, the gold of the spoil rendered to God in thanksgiving and for the covering of the soul, the truth that the mercy of preservation moves the heart to give and to seek atonement, the figure of the thank offering and the atonement of those whom God has preserved); the gold of the spoil brought as a freewill offering in gratitude (the thankful gift, the oblation) — the offering (the gold rendered); the offering to make atonement for their souls, the preserved seeking the covering of the soul (the atonement money, the thankful atonement, Exodus 30:12; 1 Peter 1:18-19) — the figure (the true atonement made by Christ, the providence, the offering, the gold received and brought to the tabernacle to follow, vv.51-54) — Clarke, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does the officers' offering of gold 'to make an atonement for our souls' signify?

sides.position: The thankful offering of those God had preserved, rendered in gratitude for the wonderful preservation and as an atonement for the soul (the numbering that called for a ransom, Exodus 30:12, 16) - a figure of the thankful giving of the saved and of the true atonement for the soul made by Christ, of which this gold was a shadow (1 Peter 1:18-19)

themes: We have therefore brought an oblation for the LORD, what every man hath gotten, of jewels of gold, chains, and bracelets, rings, earrings, and tablets, to make an atonement for our souls before the LORD
the officers' freewill offering of gold to make atonement for their souls (the crux of the thank offering and the atonement of the preserved)
the grateful gift of those God had spared, rendered in thanksgiving for the wonderful preservation and as an atonement for the soul
a figure of the thankful giving of the saved and the true atonement made by Christ (Exodus 30:12; 1 Peter 1:18-19)

schools.summary: Moses and Eleazar receiving the gold of the offering: 'and Moses and Eleazar the priest took the gold of them, even all wrought jewels' — Moses and the high priest received the gold the officers brought, all of it worked jewels (the Moses and Eleazar the priest took the gold of them, the offering received, the even all wrought jewels, the worked gold of the spoil, the figure, the offering received by Moses and the priest in God's name, the truth that the thankful gift was accepted, the gold of gratitude received, the figure of the offering of God's people accepted by his ministers, the gift owned, the gold of the spoil, rendered in thanksgiving, received for the service of God, vv.54); Moses and Eleazar receiving the gold (the offering taken) — the receiving (the gold taken); 'all wrought jewels' (the worked gold of the spoil, the offering, the figure, the gift accepted, the providence, the gold received, the weight and the memorial to follow, vv.52-54) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: And Moses and Eleazar the priest took the gold of them, even all wrought jewels
Moses and Eleazar receiving the gold of the offering
the thankful gift of the officers received by Moses and the high priest in God's name
the gold of gratitude accepted for the service of God

schools.summary: The great weight of the gold offering: 'and all the gold of the offering that they offered up to the LORD, of the captains of thousands, and of the captains of hundreds, was sixteen thousand seven hundred and fifty shekels' — the gold the captains offered came to sixteen thousand seven hundred fifty shekels, a great sum (the all the gold of the offering that they offered up to the LORD, the whole offering, the of the captains of thousands, and of the captains of hundreds, from the officers, the sixteen thousand seven hundred and fifty shekels, the great weight, the figure, the abundance of the thankful offering, a great sum of gold rendered to God, the truth that the gratitude of the preserved was great, the offering rich, the figure of the liberal giving of the thankful, the gold of the spoil rendered in abundance, the great offering owning the great mercy, the captains giving richly for their preservation); the whole offering of the captains (the gold offered) — the offering (from the officers); 'sixteen thousand seven hundred and fifty shekels' (the great weight of the gold, the rich offering, the figure, the liberal giving of the thankful, the providence, the great sum, the memorial to follow, vv.54) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And all the gold of the offering that they offered up to the LORD, of the captains of thousands, and of the captains of hundreds, was sixteen thousand seven hundred and fifty shekels
the great weight of the gold offering
the rich offering of the captains, a great sum of gold rendered to God
the abundance of gratitude for the great mercy of preservation

schools.summary: The soldiers keeping their own spoil: '(for the men of war had taken spoil, every man for himself.)' — apart from the divided spoil, each soldier had taken booty for himself, out of which the officers' offering came (the the men of war had taken spoil, the soldiers' own booty, the every man for himself, each keeping what he took, the figure, the soldiers' private spoil, distinct from the divided portion, the truth that the officers' gold offering came from their own gain, a freewill gift, the figure of the freewill offering from what is one's own, the gratitude that gives of its own spoil, the note that the offering was voluntary, from the men's own booty, not the divided tribute); the soldiers' own booty (the private spoil) — the spoil (the soldiers' own); 'every man for himself' (each soldier keeping his own booty, the private gain, the figure, the freewill offering from one's own, the providence, the soldiers' spoil, the memorial of the gold to follow, vv.54) — Gill.

themes: (For the men of war had taken spoil, every man for himself.)
the soldiers keeping their own spoil
the soldiers' private booty, distinct from the divided portion, out of which the officers' offering freely came
the freewill offering from what is one's own

schools.summary: The gold brought into the tabernacle as a memorial: 'and Moses and Eleazar the priest took the gold of the captains of thousands and of hundreds, and brought it into the tabernacle of the congregation, for a memorial for the children of Israel before the LORD' — the gold offering brought into the tabernacle as a lasting memorial of God's mercy (the Moses and Eleazar the priest took the gold of the captains, the offering received, the brought it into the tabernacle of the congregation, placed in the sanctuary, the for a memorial for the children of Israel before the LORD, the lasting remembrance, the figure, the gold laid up in the tabernacle as a memorial of the victory and the preservation, the truth that God's mercies are to be remembered, the offering a standing memorial before him, the figure of the remembrance of God's goodness, the thank offering kept as a memorial, the gold a token before the LORD of the great deliverance and the thankful hearts of his people, that his mercy be not forgotten); the gold brought into the tabernacle (the offering placed) — the placing (in the sanctuary); 'for a memorial for the children of Israel before the LORD' (the lasting remembrance of God's mercy, the memorial, the figure, the remembrance of God's goodness, the providence, the memorial, closing the war on Midian, before the request of Reuben and Gad, ch.32) — JFB, Gill.

themes: And Moses and Eleazar the priest took the gold of the captains of thousands and of hundreds, and brought it into the tabernacle of the congregation, for a memorial for the children of Israel before the LORD
the gold brought into the tabernacle as a memorial
the thankful gold laid up in the sanctuary as a standing memorial of the victory and the wonderful preservation
that God's mercy be not forgotten
